title: Myths about Emotions
source_marker: Emotion Regulation Worksheet 3 (Emotion Regulation Handout 4a) (p. 1 of 2)

Emotion Regulation Worksheet 3 (Emotion Regulation Handout 4a) (p. 1 of 2)
Myths about Emotions
Due Date: Name: Week Starting:
For each myth, write down a challenge that makes sense to you. Although the one already
written may make a lot of sense, try to come up with another one or rewrite the one there in your
own words. 1.	There is a right way to feel in every situation. Challenge: Every person responds differently to a situation. There is no correct or right way. My challenge: 2.	Letting others know that I am feeling bad is a weakness. Challenge: Letting others know that I am feeling bad is a healthy form of communication. My challenge: 3.	Negative feelings are bad and destructive. Challenge: Negative feelings are natural responses. They help me to create a better
understanding of the situation. My challenge: 4.	Being emotional means being out of control. Challenge: Being emotional means being a normal human being. My challenge: 5.	Some emotions are stupid. Challenge: Every emotion indicates how I am feeling in a certain situation. All emotions are
useful to help me understand what I am experiencing. My challenge: 6.	All painful emotions are a result of a bad attitude. Challenge: All painful emotions are natural responses to something. My challenge: 7.	If others don’t approve of my feelings, I obviously shouldn’t feel the way I do. Challenge: I have every right to feel the way I do, regardless of what other people think. My challenge: 8.	Other people are the best judges of how I am feeling. Challenge: I am the best judge of how I feel. Other people can only guess how I feel. My challenge: 9.	Painful emotions are not important and should be ignored. Challenge: Painful emotions can be warning signs telling me that a situation I am in is not
good. My challenge:
10.	Extreme emotions get you a lot further than trying to regulate your emotions. Challenge: Extreme emotions can often cause trouble for me and for other people. If an
emotion is not effective, emotion regulation is a good idea. My challenge:
(continued on next page)

280
Emotion Regulation Worksheet 3 (p. 2 of 2)
11.	Creativity requires intense, often out-of-control emotions. Challenge: I can be in control of my emotions and be creative. My challenge:
12.	Drama is cool. Challenge: I can be dramatic and regulate my emotions. My challenge:
13.	It is inauthentic to try to change my emotions. Challenge: Change is itself authentic; it is part of life. My challenge:
14.	Emotional truth is what counts, not factual truth. Challenge: Both emotional feeling and facts matter. My challenge:
15.	People should do whatever they feel like doing. Challenge: Doing what I feel like doing can be ineffective. My challenge:
16.	Acting on your emotions is the mark of a truly free individual. Challenge: The truly free person can regulate emotions. My challenge:
17.	My emotions are who I am. Challenge: Emotions are partly but not completely who I am. My challenge:
18.	My emotions are why people love me. Challenge: People will still love me if I regulate my emotions. My challenge:
19.	Emotions can just happen for no reason. Challenge: All things in the universe are caused. My challenge:
20.	Emotions should always be trusted. Challenge: Emotions should sometimes be trusted. My challenge:
21.	Other myth: Challenge: My challenge:

Emotion Regulation Worksheet 3 (Emotion Regulation Handout 4a) (p. 1 of 2)
Myths about Emotions
Due Date: Name: Week Starting:
For each myth, write down a challenge that makes sense to you. Although the one already
written may make a lot of sense, try to come up with another one or rewrite the one there in your
own words. 1.	There is a right way to feel in every situation. Challenge: Every person responds differently to a situation. There is no correct or right way. My challenge: 2.	Letting others know that I am feeling bad is a weakness. Challenge: Letting others know that I am feeling bad is a healthy form of communication. My challenge: 3.	Negative feelings are bad and destructive. Challenge: Negative feelings are natural responses. They help me to create a better
understanding of the situation. My challenge: 4.	Being emotional means being out of control. Challenge: Being emotional means being a normal human being. My challenge: 5.	Some emotions are stupid. Challenge: Every emotion indicates how I am feeling in a certain situation. All emotions are
useful to help me understand what I am experiencing. My challenge: 6.	All painful emotions are a result of a bad attitude. Challenge: All painful emotions are natural responses to something. My challenge: 7.	If others don’t approve of my feelings, I obviously shouldn’t feel the way I do. Challenge: I have every right to feel the way I do, regardless of what other people think. My challenge: 8.	Other people are the best judges of how I am feeling. Challenge: I am the best judge of how I feel. Other people can only guess how I feel. My challenge: 9. emotion is not effective, emotion regulation is a good idea. My challenge:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Myths about Emotions

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Open the file without requiring completion.
Fill one field, circle one option, or read one prompt.
Stop, save, or print; partial completion counts.

routine_integration.routine_role: weekly reflection, planning, or repair tool
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: only insert on expanded days into an empty/optional block
routine_integration.recommended_block: weekly reset, conflict repair, cleaning/planning review, or emotion review
